model.label: GPT-5.2
model.description: OpenAI • Smaller GPT frontier model tuned for fast, cost-efficient reasoning and tool use.

pick.rationale: This is essentially a toss-up between two elite teams, but Michigan has the slight overall efficiency edge in both KenPom (#2 vs. #3) and Torvik (#2 vs. #3), with a marginally better defense and comparable top-10 offense. In a one-game Final Four setting, that defensive floor plus Michigan’s ability to score efficiently in the half court is a small separator. Arizona absolutely has a credible path if it wins the shot-variance battle (especially from three) and leverages its own elite defense to speed Michigan up, so confidence stays modest.

reasoningStep.title: Final Four semifinal: lean Michigan on tiny two-way edge
reasoningStep.summary: Both teams profile as top-3 nationally, but Michigan’s slightly better adjusted margin and defense across KenPom/Torvik makes it the narrow pick in a near coin-flip.
evidence: KenPom: Michigan #2 (AdjEM 38.38; AdjO 126.7 #8; AdjD 88.4) vs Arizona #3 (AdjEM 37.56; AdjO 127.2 #7; AdjD 89.7).
Torvik: Michigan #2 (AdjO 127.67 #7; AdjD 90.51 #2) vs Arizona #3 (AdjO 126.22 #10; AdjD 91.13 #3).
Matchup logic: with offenses similarly elite, Michigan’s slightly better defensive efficiency provides a small but meaningful edge in a one-game setting.
Upset path: Arizona can win by forcing turnovers and winning the three-point variance, so confidence remains modest.

Availability context: no verified, matchup-flipping injury news surfaced in the quick scan; Michigan’s earlier Yaxel Lendeborg ankle issue was reported as a low-ankle sprain with expectation to play entering the tournament.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much cl

result.text: Depth concerns popping up for Arizona Wildcats men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues | Arizona Desert Swarm Skip to main content SB Nation SB Nation logo An Arizona Wildcats Community Arizona Desert Swarm March 17, 2026 Arizona Desert Swarm Notifications Notifications The Feed Sections Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Navigation Drawer Login / Sign Up close Close Search The Feed Sections Expand Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting Expand FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Community Guidelines Newsletters All communities Facebook Twitter Youtube RSS Arizona Desert Swarm Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues Notifications Notifications Arizona Basketball Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues by Brian Pedersen Feb 16, 2026, 10:00 AM UTC Facebook Link TUCSON, ARIZONA - FEBRUARY 14: Motiejus Krivas #13 and Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats walk off the court after losing to the Texas Tech Red Raiders 78-75 in overtime at McKale Center at ALKEME Arena on February 14, 2026 in Tucson, Arizona. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU. If Peat can’t go it will mark the first time this season the UA will send out a different starting five, but more importantly it means the 8-man rotation that has served

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much cl

result.text: Depth concerns popping up for Arizona Wildcats men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues | Arizona Desert Swarm Skip to main content SB Nation SB Nation logo An Arizona Wildcats Community Arizona Desert Swarm March 17, 2026 Arizona Desert Swarm Notifications Notifications The Feed Sections Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Navigation Drawer Login / Sign Up close Close Search The Feed Sections Expand Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting Expand FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Community Guidelines Newsletters All communities Facebook Twitter Youtube RSS Arizona Desert Swarm Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues Notifications Notifications Arizona Basketball Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues by Brian Pedersen Feb 16, 2026, 10:00 AM UTC Facebook Link TUCSON, ARIZONA - FEBRUARY 14: Motiejus Krivas #13 and Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats walk off the court after losing to the Texas Tech Red Raiders 78-75 in overtime at McKale Center at ALKEME Arena on February 14, 2026 in Tucson, Arizona. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU. If Peat can’t go it will mark the first time this season the UA will send out a different starting five, but more importantly it means the 8-man rotation that has served

In a one-game Final Four setting, that defensive floor plus Michigan’s ability to score efficiently in the half court is a small separator. Arizona absolutely has a credible path if it wins the shot-variance battle (especially from three) and leverages its own elite defense to speed Michigan up, so confidence stays modest."},"reasoningStep":{"id":"demo-2026-bracket:f4-2","title":"Final Four semifinal: lean Michigan on tiny two-way edge","summary":"Both teams profile as top-3 nationally, but Michigan’s slightly better adjusted margin and defense across KenPom/Torvik makes it the narrow pick in a near coin-flip.","evidence":["KenPom: Michigan #2 (AdjEM 38.38; AdjO 126.7 #8; AdjD 88.4) vs Arizona #3 (AdjEM 37.56; AdjO 127.2 #7; AdjD 89.7).","Torvik: Michigan #2 (AdjO 127.67 #7; AdjD 90.51 #2) vs Arizona #3 (AdjO 126.22 #10; AdjD 91.13 #3).","Matchup logic: with offenses similarly elite, Michigan’s slightly better defensive efficiency provides a small but meaningful edge in a one-game setting.","Upset path: Arizona can win by forcing turnovers and winning the three-point variance, so confidence remains modest.","Availability context: no verified, matchup-flipping injury news surfaced in the quick scan; Michigan’s earlier Yaxel Lendeborg ankle issue was reported as a low-ankle sprain with expectation to play entering the tournament."]}}
